analogy: Think of "Microbes in Human Welfare" through the chapter's own opening images: Besides macroscopic plants and animals, microbes are the major components of biological systems on this earth. You have studied about the diversity of living organisms in Class XI. Do you remember which Kingdoms among the living organisms contain micro-organisms? Which are the ones that are... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER 8, MICROBES IN HUMAN WELFARE, 8.1 Microbes in Household, 8.2 Microbes in Industrial. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Microbes in Human Welfare is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that besides macroscopic plants and animals, microbes are the major components of biological systems on this earth.

The text explains that you have studied about the diversity of living organisms in Class XI.

The text explains that do you remember which Kingdoms among the living organisms contain micro-organisms?

The text explains that which are the ones that are only microscopic?

The text explains that microbes are present everywhere – in soil, water, air, inside our bodies and that of other animals and plants.

The text explains that they are present even at sites where no other life-form could possibly exist– sites such as deep inside the geysers (thermal vents) where the temperature may be as high as 1000C, deep in the soil, under the layers of snow several metres thick, and in highly acidic environments.

The text explains that microbes are diverse– protozoa, bacteria, fungi and microscopic animal and plant viruses, viroids and also prions that are proteinacious infectious agents.

The text explains that some of the microbes are shown in Figures 8.1 and 8.2.

The text explains that microbes like bacteria and many fungi can be grown on nutritive media to form colonies (Figure 8.3), that can be seen with the naked eyes.

The text explains that such cultures are useful in studies on micro-organisms.

The text explains that cHAPTER 8 MICROBES IN HUMAN WELFARE 8.1 Microbes in Household Products 8.2 Microbes in Industrial Products 8.3 Microbes in Sewage Treatment 8.4 Microbes in Production of Biogas 8.5 Microbes as Biocontrol Agents 8.6 Microbes as Biofertilisers

The text explains that 150 BIOLOGY (a) (b) (c) (a) (b) Figure 8.3 (a) Colonies of bacteria growing in a petri dish; (b) Fungal colony growing in a petri dish (a) (b) (c) Figure 8.1 Bacteria: (a) Rod-shaped, magnified 1500X; (b) Spherical shaped, magnified1500X; (c) A rod- shaped bacterium showing flagella, magnified 50,000X Figure 8.2 Viruses: (a) A bacteriophage; (b) Adenovirus which causes respiratory infections; (c) Rod-shaped Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV).

The text explains that 151 MICROBES IN HUMAN WELFARE In chapter 7, you have read that microbes cause a large number of diseases in human beings.

The text explains that they also cause diseases in animals and plants.

The text explains that but this should not make you think that all microbes are harmful; several microbes are useful to man in diverse ways.

The text explains that some of the most important contributions of microbes to human welfare are discussed in this chapter.

The text explains that a small amount of curd added to the fresh milk as inoculum or starter contain millions of LAB, which at suitable temperatures multiply, thus converting milk to curd, which also improves its nutritional quality by increasing vitamin B12.

The text explains that depending on the type of the raw material used for fermentation and the type of processing (with or without distillation) different types of alcoholic drinks are obtained.

The text explains that 8.2.2 Antibiotics Antibiotics produced by microbes are regarded as one of the most significant discoveries of the twentieth century and have greatly contributed towards the welfare of the human society.

The text explains that can you name some other antibiotics and find out their sources?

The text explains that this cannot be discharged into natural water bodies like rivers and streams directly – you can understand why.

The text explains that this allows vigorous growth of useful aerobic microbes into flocs (masses of bacteria associated with fungal filaments to form mesh like structures).

The text explains that the BOD test measures the rate of uptake of oxygen by micro-organisms in a sample of water and thus, indirectly, BOD is a measure of the organic matter present in the water.

The text explains that these gases form biogas and can be used as source of energy as it is inflammable.

The text explains that so the untreated sewage is often discharged directly into rivers leading to their pollution and increase in water-borne diseases.

The text explains that in modern society, these problems have been tackled increasingly by the use of chemicals – by use of insecticides and pesticides.

The text explains that the organic farmer, therefore, works to create a system where the insects that are sometimes called pests are not eradicated, but instead are kept at manageable levels by a complex system of checks and balances within a living and vibrant ecosystem.

The text explains that you will learn more about this in chapter 10.

The text explains that trichoderma species are free-living fungi that are very common in the root ecosystems.

The text explains that of course, we have now realised that there are problems associated with the overuse of chemical fertilisers and there is a large pressure to switch to organic farming – the use of biofertilisers.

The text explains that the main sources of biofertilisers are bacteria, fungi and cyanobacteria.

The text explains that sUMMARY Microbes are a very important component of life on earth.

The text explains that microbes are used to produce industrial products like lactic acid, acetic acid and alcohol, which are used in a variety of processes in the industry.

The text explains that do you think microbes can also be used as source of energy?

The text explains that for more than a hundred years, microbes are being used to treat sewage (waste water) by the process of activated sludge formation and this helps in recycling of water in nature.

The text explains that biogas produced by microbes is used as a source of energy in rural areas.

The text explains that microbes can also be used to kill harmful pests, a process called as biocontrol.

The text explains that it is clear from the diverse uses human beings have put microbes to that they play an important role in the welfare of human society.

The text explains that arrange the following in the decreasing order (most important first) of their importance, for the welfare of human society.

The text explains that methanogens produce methane (biogas) while degrading plant waste.

The text explains that the biocontrol measures help us to avoid heavy use of toxic pesticides for controlling pests.

The text explains that there is a need these days to push for use of biofertilisers in place of chemical fertilisers.

The text explains that find out the name of the microbes from which Cyclosporin A (an immunosuppressive drug) and Statins (blood cholesterol lowering agents) are obtained.

The text explains that find out the role of microbes in the following and discuss it with your teacher.

The text explains that how do biofertilisers enrich the fertility of the soil?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8: Microbes in Human Welfare

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER 8 and MICROBES IN HUMAN WELFARE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Microbes in Human Welfare; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Microbes in Human Welfare through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER 8 and MICROBES IN HUMAN WELFARE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

options: Some of the most important contributions of microbes to human welfare are discussed in this chapter.
8.1 MICROBES IN HOUSEHOLD PRODUCTS You would be surprised to know that we use microbes or products derived from them everyday.
Micro-organisms such as Lactobacillus and others commonly called lactic acid bacteria (LAB) grow in milk and convert it to curd.
During growth, the LAB produce acids that coagulate and partially digest the milk proteins.
